fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this page-processing library thing that the data pipeline folks have been asking about for a while. Basically the problem is that right now every team that wants to work with column pages has to write their own decoding logic before they can do any kind of row filtering or value extraction, and they keep getting it wrong especially when those weird compressed page formats come into play. We want one consistent interface they can all use.

The library needs to handle at least three kinds of page layouts — you know, the normal one, the one where every row is the same value, and the dictionary-compressed one. For the dictionary case specifically there's some tricky fallback behavior we discussed in the arch review last month that needs to be baked in.

It should support two main operations: picking which rows pass a condition (either a numeric window check or a remainder-based check, same ones we used in the billing filter module), and then pulling out the actual values for a given set of rows. Row sets can be described either as a straight range or as an explicit list.

The whole thing needs a CLI adapter that reads JSON from stdin and writes results to stdout so the test harness can drive it. There's also some metadata reporting that pipeline tooling depends on — make sure that part is stable. Please follow the same separation pattern we used on the auth refactor.

One small follow-up after the questions that came in: for the range-style filter, the rule is strictly greater than 3 and strictly less than 11, so 3 and 11 do not count. In practice that means the passing values are 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10. And when filter_range is false, we use the modulo version instead, which is value modulo 3 equals 1. So values 1, 4, 7, 10, 13 would pass there.

Also worth being extra clear on the positions input since a few folks asked. There are two supported shapes. The range form uses offset and size, with offset being the zero-based starting row index and size being the number of rows to include, which means it selects rows offset through offset+size-1 inclusive. The other form is just an explicit array of zero-based row position integers, and those should be projected in exactly the order they appear. The output values should follow that same order too.

On the metadata side, the filter component should output component=filter, deterministic=true, input_channels=[3]. For the projection component it’s those same three fields plus output_type=bigint. The input_channels value is always the literal list [3] for both components, and there is no pass/fail word in metadata output.

Last thing on dictionary pages because that fallback behavior is easy to misunderstand: if all dictionary entries are valid, projection reports result_encoding=dictionary. If there are invalid entries, meaning negative values, but none of the selected rows actually point at those bad entries, projection should still succeed, just materialize the referenced values as a plain result and report result_encoding=plain. Same idea for dictionary pages that contain unused invalid (negative) entries — fall back to plain result encoding and report result_encoding=plain in stdout rather than result_encoding=dictionary. If any selected rows do reference invalid entries, that should be treated as an error.

trigger_keywords: how is a repeated page projected
what does repeated result encoding look like in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the input page encoding is 'repeated', the projection result reports result_encoding=repeated. However, the rendered values list in stdout still contains the repeated value once per selected output row — it is not compressed to a single value in the output text. For example, selecting 5 rows from a repeated-7 page outputs values=[7, 7, 7, 7, 7].

trigger_keywords: empty ids list in dictionary page filter
what happens when ids array is empty
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a dictionary-encoded page has an empty ids list (zero rows), the filter operation outputs selected_positions=[] and selected_count=0. No error is raised.

trigger_keywords: how to run the test suite
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness is run via 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. It accepts an optional '--cases-dir <subdir>' argument defaulting to 'test_cases'. For each case in each *.json file it writes the raw program stdout to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_3_digit_index>.txt. For example the first case in feature1_metadata.json run with --cases-dir public_test_cases writes to rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_metadata@000.txt. Output files contain only raw stdout with no PASS/FAIL wording.

trigger_keywords: unused invalid dictionary entries that are not referenced
dictionary contains bad values but selected rows are fine
how to handle dictionary with mixed valid and invalid entries
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a dictionary contains invalid (negative) entries that are not referenced by any row id in the ids array, those entries are ignored for the purpose of both filtering and projection. Filtering evaluates only the logical values actually referenced by ids. Projection over such a page materializes only the referenced valid values and reports result_encoding=plain as the fallback signal.

trigger_keywords: how should the adapter be structured
business logic decoupled from stdin stdout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The core domain logic must have zero dependency on stdin, stdout, or JSON. A separate execution adapter layer handles reading JSON from stdin, translating it into method calls on the core system, and formatting results to stdout. The core must expose an idiomatic API (not raw string methods). Parsing, routing, core execution, and output formatting should be distinct logical units. The project warrants a multi-file structure with src/ and tests/ directories given its multiple distinct responsibilities.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same ones we used in the billing filter module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Two specific predicates: filter_range=true means value > 3 AND value < 11; filter_range=false means value % 3 == 1

context_pointers.vague_reference: same separation pattern we used on the auth refactor
context_pointers.actual_meaning: SOLID multi-file architecture with strict separation: core domain logic in src/, execution adapter as a separate entry point, JSON I/O never touching core business logic classes

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify off the top of my head — could you check the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or look at how the expected_output strings are structured? The answers to most edge cases should be derivable from those examples.

feature1_metadata.json.description: Reports stable execution metadata for the row-selection and value-projection components.

feature2_filter_plain_and_repeated.json.description: Selects row positions from plain and repeated numeric pages using either an inclusive numeric window or a modular predicate.

feature3_filter_dictionary.json.description: Selects row positions from dictionary-encoded numeric pages, including partial matches, empty pages, all-match dictionaries, and dictionaries with unused invalid entries.

feature4_project_plain_and_repeated.json.description: Projects selected numeric values from plain and repeated pages while preserving row order and reporting the resulting page encoding.

feature5_project_dictionary.json.description: Projects selected numeric values from dictionary-encoded pages, preserving selected row order and falling back to a plain page when unused dictionary entries are invalid.
